perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that its obligations here under are necessary and reasonable in order to protect Sanmina and Sanmina’s business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages will be inadequate to compensate Sanmina for any breach by either party of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such viola tion or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to Sanmina and that, in addition to any other remedies th at may be avail able, in law, in equity or otherwise, Sanmina will be entitled to seek injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages and without the necessity of posting bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that its obligations here under are necessary and reasonable in order to protect Sanmina and Sanmina’s business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages will be inadequate to compensate Sanmina for any breach by either party of any covenants and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such viola tion or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to Sanmina and that, in addition to any other remedies th at may be avail able, in law, in equity or otherwise, Sanmina will be entitled to seek injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'without the necessity of posting bond' impacts Sanmina's ability to obtain injunctive relief easily. California law often requires a bond unless specifically waived by statute or agreement. Removing this waiver may create an undue burden.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 529 generally requires a bond to be posted before an injunction takes effect, to cover damages the enjoined party might suffer if the injunction is later found to be wrongly issued. By deleting the waiver of the necessity of posting a bond, the altered agreement introduces legal risk by requiring Sanmina to potentially post a bond to obtain injunctive relief, which they explicitly waived in the original text.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 529 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 529. (a) On granting an injunction, the court or judge must require an undertaking on the part of the applicant to the effect that the applicant will pay to the party enjoined any damages, not exceeding an amount to be specified, the party may sustain by reason of the injunction, if the court finally decides that the applicant was not entitled to the injunction. Within five days after the service of the injunction, the person enjoined may object to the undertaking. If the court determines that the applicant’s undertaking is insufficient and a sufficient undertaking is not filed within the time required by statute, the order granting the injunction must be dissolved. (b) This section does not apply to any of the following persons: (1) Either spouse against the other in a proceeding for legal separation or dissolution of marriage. (2) The applicant for an order described in Division 10 (commencing with Section 6200) of the Family Code. (3) A public entity or officer described in Section 995.220.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit waiver of the bond requirement for injunctive relief. The original text stated Sanmina could seek injunctive relief 'without the necessity of posting bond'. CCP 529(a) states that 'On granting an injunction, the court or judge must require an undertaking on the part of the applicant'. By removing the waiver, the modified text implies Sanmina may now be required to post a bond, which directly contradicts the initial agreement and introduces a potential legal hurdle that was explicitly waived.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California and the federal courts located in the Northern District of the State of California shall have exclusive jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. The parties consent to the exercise by such courts of personal jurisdiction over them and each party waives any objection it might otherwise have to venue, personal jurisdiction, inconvenience of forum, and any similar or related doctrine.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that the state courts of Santa Clara County, California shall have jurisdiction and venue to adjudicate any and all disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. The parties consent to the exercise by such courts of personal jurisdiction over them and each party waives any objection it might otherwise have to venue, personal jurisdiction, inconvenience of forum, and any similar or related doctrine.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the federal court jurisdiction clause, the agreement now prevents Sanmina from potentially litigating in federal court, even if diversity jurisdiction exists. This eliminates a venue option that could be strategically advantageous for Sanmina depending on the specifics of the case.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification restricts the venue to only state courts. It contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332, which grants federal courts jurisdiction over civil actions where the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 and is between citizens of different states. Because the original agreement allowed for federal jurisdiction, and the modified one does not, it impacts Sanmina's legal options depending on the recipient's location and potential legal claims.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the option for disputes to be adjudicated in federal courts, which directly contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1332 as shown in both scraped snippets. This statute grants federal courts original jurisdiction over civil actions exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states. By limiting jurisdiction to state courts, the modified agreement eliminates a venue option available under federal law, thus undermining the rights provided by 28 U.S.C. § 1332.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges its obligations to control access to technical data under the U.S. export laws and regulations and agrees to adhere to such laws and regulations with regard to any technical data rec eived under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges its obligations regarding technical data rec eived under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to U.S. export laws and regulations weakens the recipient's acknowledgement of their responsibility to comply with these laws. This increases the risk of non-compliance, especially if the recipient is located outside the U.S.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific mention of U.S. export laws (EAR) and regulations, the changed text creates a potential legal risk. The EAR, codified in 15 CFR Parts 730-774, governs the export and re-export of certain goods, software, and technology. The original clause served to remind the Recipient of their obligations under these regulations. The omission reduces awareness and may lead to non-compliance with US export control laws.

Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the explicit reference to "U.S. export laws and regulations" creates a legal contradiction. The original text clearly acknowledges the recipient's obligations under U.S. export laws, while the changed text only mentions obligations regarding technical data without specifying the governing laws. This weakens the recipient's acknowledgment of and responsibility to comply with these laws (EAR), potentially leading to non-compliance. The 'Law Explanation' states that the omission reduces awareness and may lead to non-compliance with U.S. export control laws, indicating a clear weakening of the legal obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. By removing the specific mention of U.S. export laws, the contract increases the risk of violating these regulations, as the recipient may be unaware of the specific legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: This document contains the complete and exclusive Agreement between the parties, and it is intended to be final expression of their agreement. No promise, representation, warranty or covenant not included in this document has been or is relied upon by any party. Each party has relied upon its own examination of the warranties, representations and covenants expressly contained in the Agreement itself. No modification or amendment of this Agreement shall be o f any force unless in writing executed by all parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This document contains the complete Agreement between the parties, and it is intended to be final expression of their agreement. No promise, representation, warranty or covenant not included in this document has been or is relied upon by any party. Each party has relied upon its own examination of the warranties, representations and covenants contained in the Agreement itself. No modification or amendment of this Agreement shall be o f any force unless agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'exclusive' and changing 'in writing executed by all parties' to 'agreed upon by both parties', it introduces the opportunity for verbal agreements to be used, even though the agreement should not be modified unless written and executed by both parties. This change may open a loophole for enforceability, especially in California where parol evidence rules are complex.
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of 'exclusive' and changing the modification clause to 'agreed upon by both parties' weakens the integration clause. California Civil Code § 1625 states that a written contract supersedes all prior or contemporaneous oral negotiations or stipulations concerning its matter. By omitting 'exclusive', and changing the modification clause, the revised agreement could arguably allow for modifications or interpretations based on prior oral agreements or understandings, potentially contradicting the principle that the written contract should be the sole source of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1625 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1625. The execution of a contract in writing, whether the law requires it to be written or not, supersedes all the negotiations or stipulations concerning its matter which preceded or accompanied the execution of the instrument. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 451.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1625. The original contract specified that modifications must be 'in writing executed by all parties,' providing a clear and strict requirement. The modified text changes this to 'agreed upon by both parties,' which opens the door to verbal agreements, contradicting the principle in CIV 1625 that a written contract supersedes all prior or contemporaneous oral negotiations. The omission of 'exclusive' and the change in the modification clause, as explained, weakens the integration clause, potentially allowing for interpretations based on prior oral agreements. This directly violates the provision in the snippet that states the written contract supersedes all negotiations.
